course_info.title: Cryptography and Data Protection
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Trace the evolution of cryptography from classical ciphers to modern algorithms
Perform modular arithmetic operations underlying cryptographic algorithms
Identify the properties required for a cryptographic system to be considered secure
Apply Shannon's confusion and diffusion principles to evaluate cipher design
readings: Singh, The Code Book, Ch. 1-3 (historical context)

lecture_notes.introduction: Cryptography is the science of securing information through mathematical transformation. For millennia, humans have needed to communicate secretly: from Caesar's shifted alphabet used to coordinate Roman legions, to the Enigma machine that shaped World War II, to the RSA algorithm that secures trillions of dollars in online commerce today. What changed is the mathematical rigor. Modern cryptography rests on computational hardness assumptions: problems that are easy to compute in one direction but infeasible to reverse without a secret key. Before diving into algorithms, we must build the mathematical toolkit. Modular arithmetic, prime numbers, finite fields, and group theory are the language of cryptography. Shannon's 1949 paper established that a secure cipher must exhibit both confusion (obscuring the relationship between key and ciphertext) and diffusion (spreading plaintext statistics across the ciphertext). Kerckhoffs' principle -- that a system should be secure even if everything except the key is public -- remains the foundation of all modern cryptographic design. This course will take you from these foundations through every major cryptographic primitive used in modern security systems.
key_concepts: Classical ciphers: Caesar (shift), Vigenere (polyalphabetic), Enigma (rotor machine), and why each was broken
Kerckhoffs' principle: security must depend on the secrecy of the key, not the secrecy of the algorithm
Modular arithmetic: addition, multiplication, and exponentiation in Z_n, computing inverses with the Extended Euclidean Algorithm
Prime numbers: primality testing (Miller-Rabin), prime generation for key creation
Finite fields GF(2^8): the mathematical structure underlying AES byte operations
Shannon's confusion and diffusion: confusion hides key-ciphertext relationship, diffusion spreads plaintext influence
Computational security: an algorithm is secure if breaking it requires more computation than any adversary can perform
One-time pad: provably secure (information-theoretic security) but impractical due to key length requirement equal to message length
Frequency analysis: counting letter frequencies to break substitution ciphers, the first cryptanalytic technique
lecture_notes.demonstration: Break a monoalphabetic substitution cipher using frequency analysis in Python. Count character frequencies in the ciphertext, map them to English letter frequencies, and iteratively refine the mapping. Then encrypt the same plaintext with a one-time pad and show that frequency analysis reveals nothing. Finally, demonstrate modular exponentiation efficiency: compute 7^256 mod 13 using repeated squaring in milliseconds versus naive exponentiation that would take longer than the age of the universe.
setup_requirements: Python 3 with PyCryptodome library installed (pip install pycryptodome)
CyberChef web tool for encoding/decoding exercises
Mathematical notation reference sheet for modular arithmetic
Jupyter notebook environment for interactive demonstrations

review.value: A cryptosystem should be secure even if everything about the system except the key is public knowledge. This is why modern algorithms (AES, RSA) are fully published and studied by thousands of cryptographers, yet remain secure. Security through obscurity is not security.

Confusion obscures the relationship between key and ciphertext, typically achieved through substitution (S-boxes). Diffusion spreads plaintext statistics across the ciphertext, typically achieved through permutation and mixing operations. AES achieves both in every round.

The only cipher with proven perfect secrecy. Key must be truly random, at least as long as the message, used only once, and kept secret. Any violation of these conditions destroys its security. Used in practice only for the most sensitive communications.

Information-theoretic security (one-time pad) holds regardless of computational power. Computational security (AES, RSA) assumes the adversary has bounded computing resources. All practical modern cryptography relies on computational security.

learning_objectives: Explain the internal structure and round operations of DES and AES
Understand why DES is insecure and how 3DES extends its life
Trace a single round of AES: SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey
Select appropriate key sizes for different security requirements
readings: NIST FIPS 197: Advanced Encryption Standard specification
Daemen & Rijmen, The Design of Rijndael, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Symmetric encryption is the workhorse of data confidentiality. The same key encrypts and decrypts, making it fast and efficient for bulk data. DES (Data Encryption Standard), published in 1977, dominated for two decades with its 56-bit key and 16-round Feistel network. But its short key length became its downfall: in 1998, the EFF's Deep Crack machine brute-forced a DES key in 56 hours for $250,000, and today it takes seconds on a GPU cluster. Triple DES (3DES) applies DES three times with different keys (encrypt-decrypt-encrypt), providing 112-bit effective security but at triple the computational cost. AES (Advanced Encryption Standard), selected through a public competition won by Rijndael in 2001, replaced DES as the standard. AES operates on 128-bit blocks using a substitution-permutation network rather than a Feistel structure. Its four round operations -- SubBytes (non-linear substitution via S-box), ShiftRows (byte permutation), MixColumns (column mixing in GF(2^8)), and AddRoundKey (XOR with round key) -- achieve excellent confusion and diffusion. Understanding AES internals is essential because it appears everywhere: TLS, disk encryption, Wi-Fi security, VPNs, and database encryption.
key_concepts: DES: 64-bit block, 56-bit key, 16-round Feistel network, S-boxes for non-linearity, broken by brute force due to small key space
3DES: EDE (Encrypt-Decrypt-Encrypt) with 2 or 3 keys, 112-bit or 168-bit effective security, 3x slower than DES
AES block size: 128 bits (16 bytes arranged as 4x4 byte matrix)
AES key sizes: AES-128 (10 rounds), AES-192 (12 rounds), AES-256 (14 rounds)
SubBytes: non-linear byte substitution using a 16x16 S-box derived from multiplicative inverse in GF(2^8)
ShiftRows: cyclic left shift of each row by 0, 1, 2, 3 positions, provides diffusion across columns
MixColumns: matrix multiplication over GF(2^8), mixes bytes within each column for diffusion
AddRoundKey: XOR the state with the round key derived from the key schedule
Key schedule: derives round keys from the master key using RotWord, SubWord, and XOR with round constants
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement AES-128 encryption of a single block step by step in Python, printing the state matrix after each operation in every round. Visualize how SubBytes provides confusion (small input change causes large output change) and how ShiftRows + MixColumns provide diffusion (one byte change propagates to all 16 bytes within 2 rounds). Compare encryption speed of DES, 3DES, and AES on 1GB of data.
setup_requirements: Python 3 with PyCryptodome for comparison benchmarks
Custom AES implementation script for step-by-step visualization
AES S-box lookup table printout
GF(2^8) multiplication reference

review.value: DES has 2^56 = 72 quadrillion possible keys. EFF's Deep Crack (1998) tested 90 billion keys/second. Modern GPUs can test trillions per second. A DES key can be brute-forced in under an hour with specialized hardware.

The best known attack on AES-128 is a biclique attack requiring 2^126.1 operations -- essentially no practical improvement over brute force. AES-256 provides even larger margin for long-term security requirements.

AES-256 is ~40% slower than AES-128 due to 4 extra rounds. AES-128 provides 128 bits of security, which is considered secure until approximately 2060. AES-256 is recommended for classified data and post-quantum preparedness.

DES uses a Feistel network (split block, process half, swap). AES uses a Substitution-Permutation Network (process entire block). SPNs allow parallelization of both encryption and decryption, making AES faster on modern CPUs with AES-NI instructions.

class_schedule.topic: Block Cipher Modes: ECB, CBC, CTR, and GCM
learning_objectives: Explain why raw block cipher encryption (ECB) is insufficient for most applications
Compare block cipher modes: ECB, CBC, CTR, GCM for different use cases
Identify the security requirements for IVs and nonces in each mode
Implement authenticated encryption with AES-GCM
readings: NIST SP 800-38A: Recommendation for Block Cipher Modes of Operation
NIST SP 800-38D: Recommendation for GCM Mode

lecture_notes.introduction: A block cipher like AES encrypts exactly one 128-bit block at a time. Real messages are longer than 128 bits, so we need modes of operation that extend block ciphers to arbitrary-length messages. The simplest mode, ECB (Electronic Codebook), encrypts each block independently with the same key. This is catastrophically insecure for structured data because identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks, revealing patterns. The famous ECB penguin image demonstrates this visually: encrypting a bitmap image with ECB preserves the outline of the image in the ciphertext. CBC (Cipher Block Chaining) solves this by XORing each plaintext block with the previous ciphertext block before encryption, but it is sequential (cannot parallelize encryption) and vulnerable to padding oracle attacks if error messages reveal padding validity. CTR (Counter) mode turns the block cipher into a stream cipher by encrypting sequential counter values and XORing with plaintext, enabling full parallelization. GCM (Galois/Counter Mode) adds authentication to CTR mode, providing Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD). GCM is the gold standard for modern applications and is mandatory in TLS 1.3.
key_concepts: ECB mode: each block encrypted independently, deterministic, reveals patterns in structured data -- never use for multi-block data
CBC mode: C_i = E_K(P_i XOR C_{i-1}), requires random unpredictable IV, sequential encryption but parallel decryption
CBC padding: PKCS#7 padding adds bytes equal to the number of padding bytes needed, padding oracle attacks exploit error messages
CTR mode: C_i = P_i XOR E_K(nonce || counter_i), fully parallelizable encryption and decryption, nonce must never repeat with same key
GCM mode: CTR encryption plus GHASH polynomial authentication over GF(2^128), provides confidentiality + integrity + authenticity
AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data): encrypts plaintext and authenticates both ciphertext and unencrypted header data
IV/Nonce requirements: CBC needs random unpredictable IV (128 bits), GCM needs unique nonce (96 bits), CTR needs unique nonce
Nonce reuse catastrophe: reusing a nonce in GCM reveals the authentication key and allows plaintext recovery via XOR of ciphertext streams
Mode selection guidance: use AES-GCM for most applications, AES-CBC only with HMAC (encrypt-then-MAC), never use ECB
lecture_notes.demonstration: Encrypt a bitmap image with AES-ECB and AES-CBC to visually demonstrate pattern leakage. Then implement AES-GCM encryption in Python: show that the same plaintext with different nonces produces completely different ciphertext, and that tampering with either the ciphertext or the associated data causes authentication failure. Finally, demonstrate the catastrophic effect of nonce reuse in GCM by encrypting two messages with the same nonce and recovering both plaintexts.
setup_requirements: Python with PyCryptodome (AES, modes)
Bitmap image files for ECB pattern demonstration
OpenSSL command-line tools for comparison
Hex editor for examining ciphertext structures

review.value: Encrypting a bitmap image with ECB preserves the outline because identical pixel blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks. This is the most intuitive demonstration of why ECB leaks information about plaintext structure.

If the same nonce is used twice with the same key in GCM, XORing the two ciphertexts cancels the keystream, revealing XOR of the plaintexts. The GHASH authentication key is also recoverable, allowing forgery of arbitrary messages. This is why nonce management is critical.

In CBC mode, if a server reveals whether decrypted padding is valid (different error for bad padding vs bad data), an attacker can decrypt any ciphertext one byte at a time by manipulating the previous ciphertext block. TLS had this vulnerability (POODLE attack).

When using CBC without built-in authentication, always apply MAC after encryption (encrypt-then-MAC). MAC-then-encrypt leaks information through padding errors. Encrypt-and-MAC leaks information through MAC comparisons. GCM avoids this issue entirely by combining encryption and authentication.

learning_objectives: Explain the design principles of stream ciphers versus block ciphers
Analyze the ChaCha20 stream cipher and its security properties
Understand the dangers of keystream reuse in stream ciphers
Compare stream cipher applications: WPA2 (RC4 weakness), TLS 1.3 (ChaCha20-Poly1305)
readings: Bernstein, The Salsa20 Family of Stream Ciphers
RFC 8439: ChaCha20 and Poly1305 for IETF Protocols

lecture_notes.introduction: Stream ciphers encrypt data one byte (or bit) at a time by XORing plaintext with a pseudorandom keystream generated from a key and nonce. Unlike block ciphers that process fixed-size blocks, stream ciphers handle arbitrary-length data naturally without padding. The critical security requirement is that the keystream must never repeat: if the same keystream is used to encrypt two messages, XORing the ciphertexts cancels the keystream and reveals the XOR of the plaintexts, which can be separated using statistical techniques. RC4 was the dominant stream cipher for decades, used in WPA (Wi-Fi) and early TLS, but multiple biases in its output led to practical attacks. ChaCha20, designed by Daniel Bernstein, replaced RC4 as the modern standard. It uses a 256-bit key and 96-bit nonce, operates on 512-bit blocks using add-rotate-XOR operations, and is resistant to timing attacks because it avoids data-dependent branches and table lookups. Paired with the Poly1305 authenticator, ChaCha20-Poly1305 provides AEAD and is mandatory in TLS 1.3 alongside AES-GCM. It is preferred on platforms without AES hardware acceleration (mobile devices, embedded systems) because its software implementation is faster than software AES.
key_concepts: Stream cipher model: C = P XOR KeyStream(K, Nonce), decryption is identical: P = C XOR KeyStream(K, Nonce)
Keystream reuse vulnerability: if C1 = P1 XOR KS and C2 = P2 XOR KS, then C1 XOR C2 = P1 XOR P2, leaking both plaintexts
RC4: PRGA generates keystream from a permutation of 256 bytes, known biases in early output bytes and long-term correlations
RC4 attacks: WPA-TKIP attack recovers plaintext from ~2^24 packets, TLS NOMORE attack recovers cookies from HTTPS traffic
ChaCha20: quarter-round function applies add, XOR, and rotate to 4 words, 20 rounds (10 column rounds + 10 diagonal rounds)
ChaCha20 state: 4x4 matrix of 32-bit words initialized from constant, key (256 bits), counter (32 bits), nonce (96 bits)
Poly1305: one-time authenticator using polynomial evaluation over GF(2^130-5), produces 128-bit authentication tag
ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD: encrypt with ChaCha20, authenticate ciphertext and AAD with Poly1305 using a derived one-time key
Performance comparison: ChaCha20 is 3-5x faster than AES in software on ARM processors without AES-NI extensions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a simplified stream cipher XOR encryption, then demonstrate keystream reuse by encrypting two English messages with the same key and nonce, XORing the ciphertexts, and using crib dragging to recover both plaintexts. Then benchmark ChaCha20-Poly1305 vs AES-256-GCM on a mobile ARM processor and a desktop x86 processor with AES-NI to show the performance crossover.
setup_requirements: Python with PyCryptodome for ChaCha20 and AES benchmarks
Crib dragging tool or script for keystream reuse demonstration
OpenSSL speed command for cross-platform benchmarking
Access to ARM-based system or emulator for performance comparison

review.value: This is the cardinal sin of stream cipher usage. A nonce must never be reused with the same key. For ChaCha20 with a 96-bit random nonce, the birthday bound gives a collision probability of 50% after 2^48 messages -- safe for most applications but dangerous for very high-volume systems.

RFC 7465 (2015) prohibited RC4 in all TLS versions. The biases in RC4 output allowed recovery of encrypted cookies and passwords from HTTPS sessions. This is a cautionary tale about using ciphers with known theoretical weaknesses in production.

Uses only add, rotate, and XOR operations (ARX design). No S-boxes or table lookups means no cache-timing side channels. Constant-time by design. This makes it inherently resistant to timing attacks that plagued AES software implementations.

Modern cryptographic best practice requires all encryption to be authenticated. Using encryption without authentication (plain AES-CBC, raw ChaCha20) allows bit-flipping attacks where an attacker modifies ciphertext to predictably alter plaintext. AEAD modes detect all tampering.

class_schedule.topic: Hash Functions: MD5, SHA-2, and SHA-3
learning_objectives: Explain the three security properties of cryptographic hash functions
Compare the internal structure of MD5, SHA-256, and SHA-3 (Keccak)
Demonstrate why MD5 and SHA-1 are broken for collision resistance
Apply hash functions for integrity verification, password storage, and digital fingerprinting
readings: Stevens et al., The First Collision for Full SHA-1 (SHAttered, 2017)

lecture_notes.introduction: Cryptographic hash functions are one-way compression functions that map arbitrary-length input to a fixed-length output (digest). They are fundamental to almost every cryptographic protocol: digital signatures hash the message before signing, password storage hashes passwords before storing, file integrity verification compares hashes, blockchain proof-of-work requires finding hash preimages. Three properties define a secure hash function. Pre-image resistance: given a hash h, it is computationally infeasible to find any message m such that H(m) = h. Second pre-image resistance: given a message m1, it is infeasible to find a different m2 with H(m1) = H(m2). Collision resistance: it is infeasible to find any two different messages m1 and m2 with H(m1) = H(m2). MD5 (128-bit output) and SHA-1 (160-bit output) are broken for collision resistance -- practical collisions have been demonstrated. SHA-2 (SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512) remains secure and is the current standard. SHA-3 (Keccak), based on a completely different sponge construction rather than the Merkle-Damgard construction used by MD5/SHA-1/SHA-2, provides an alternative if SHA-2 is ever weakened.
key_concepts: Pre-image resistance: given h, cannot find m such that H(m) = h (one-wayness)
Second pre-image resistance: given m1, cannot find m2 != m1 such that H(m1) = H(m2)
Collision resistance: cannot find any m1 != m2 such that H(m1) = H(m2) -- hardest property to maintain
Birthday attack: collision search requires approximately 2^(n/2) hash evaluations for an n-bit hash (birthday paradox)
MD5: 128-bit output, Merkle-Damgard construction, 64 rounds, BROKEN -- collisions found in seconds on a laptop (2004)
SHA-1: 160-bit output, broken in 2017 (SHAttered) with 2^63 operations, practical collision with two different PDF files producing same hash
SHA-256: 256-bit output, 64 rounds of compression using message schedule, constants from cube roots of primes, no known practical attacks
SHA-3 (Keccak): sponge construction with 1600-bit state, absorb phase (XOR input blocks) and squeeze phase (output hash blocks)
Hash function applications: password hashing (use bcrypt/argon2, not raw SHA), file integrity (SHA-256 checksums), commit signing (Git uses SHA-1, migrating to SHA-256)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute SHA-256 hashes of similar inputs to demonstrate the avalanche effect (changing one bit of input changes approximately 50% of output bits). Generate an MD5 collision using the fastcoll tool, producing two different files with the same MD5 hash. Show the SHAttered PDF collision for SHA-1. Then benchmark SHA-256 vs SHA-3-256 vs BLAKE3 for throughput and discuss selection criteria.
setup_requirements: Python with hashlib for hash computation
fastcoll tool for MD5 collision generation
SHAttered collision PDF files (downloaded from shattered.io)
openssl dgst for command-line hashing and benchmarking

review.value: A good hash function exhibits the strict avalanche criterion: changing one bit of input changes each output bit with 50% probability. SHA-256 achieves this after 2-3 rounds. This ensures that similar inputs produce completely uncorrelated outputs.

MD5 collisions can be generated in seconds. This has been exploited to create rogue CA certificates (Flame malware, 2012) and forge digital signatures. MD5 should never be used for any security purpose. It remains acceptable only as a non-cryptographic checksum for data integrity against accidental corruption.

SHA-3's sponge construction is fundamentally different from Merkle-Damgard (used by MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2). If a Merkle-Damgard weakness is discovered that affects SHA-2, SHA-3 would remain unaffected. This diversity is the primary reason NIST standardized SHA-3.

Never store passwords as raw SHA-256 hashes. SHA-256 is fast by design (billions per second on GPU). Use bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 which are deliberately slow (hundreds of milliseconds per hash) and include salt. Argon2id is the current recommendation.

learning_objectives: Explain the purpose and security model of Message Authentication Codes
Derive the HMAC construction and understand its security proof
Compare HMAC with CMAC and Poly1305 for different applications
Identify common MAC misuse patterns and their consequences
readings: RFC 2104: HMAC - Keyed-Hashing for Message Authentication
Bellare et al., Keying Hash Functions for Message Authentication (1996)

lecture_notes.introduction: A Message Authentication Code (MAC) provides integrity and authentication for messages using a shared secret key. Unlike a hash function which anyone can compute, a MAC requires knowledge of the key, so a valid MAC proves the message was created (or approved) by someone who knows the key and has not been modified in transit. HMAC (Hash-based MAC) is the most widely used MAC construction, combining a hash function with a secret key in a way that is provably secure under reasonable assumptions about the underlying hash function. The construction is: HMAC(K, M) = H((K XOR opad) || H((K XOR ipad) || M)), where ipad and opad are fixed padding constants. This nested hashing structure prevents length-extension attacks that would compromise a naive H(K || M) construction. CMAC (Cipher-based MAC) uses a block cipher (typically AES) instead of a hash function. Poly1305 is a one-time MAC designed for high performance when paired with ChaCha20 or AES. Understanding when to use each and how to use them correctly is essential for building secure protocols.
key_concepts: MAC security model: given many (message, MAC) pairs, an attacker cannot forge a valid MAC for any new message
HMAC construction: HMAC(K, M) = H((K XOR opad) || H((K XOR ipad) || M)), where ipad = 0x36 repeated and opad = 0x5C repeated
Length extension attack: for Merkle-Damgard hashes, knowing H(M) allows computing H(M || padding || extension) without knowing M, breaking naive H(K || M) MAC
HMAC security proof: HMAC is secure if the underlying hash function's compression function is a PRF
CMAC (NIST SP 800-38B): AES-based MAC using CBC-MAC with corrections for variable-length messages
Poly1305: polynomial evaluation MAC over GF(2^130-5), one-time key per message, extremely fast
MAC verification: always use constant-time comparison to prevent timing side-channel attacks
Encrypt-then-MAC vs MAC-then-Encrypt: encrypt-then-MAC is provably secure; MAC-then-encrypt has caused vulnerabilities (TLS padding oracle)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement HMAC-SHA256 from scratch in Python (using only the SHA-256 hash function) and verify it matches the library implementation. Demonstrate a length extension attack against a naive H(key || message) MAC using the hash_extender tool. Then show constant-time vs early-exit MAC comparison and measure the timing difference that enables a side-channel attack.
setup_requirements: Python with hashlib and hmac libraries
hash_extender tool for length extension attack demonstration
Timing measurement script for side-channel demonstration
Wireshark for observing HMAC in TLS handshake

review.value: For Merkle-Damgard hashes (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256), knowing H(M) and the length of M allows computing H(M || padding || extension) without knowing M. This breaks H(key || message) as a MAC because an attacker can append data and compute a valid tag. HMAC's nested structure prevents this.

If MAC comparison returns false at the first differing byte, an attacker can determine the correct MAC one byte at a time by measuring response latency. Always use constant-time comparison: compare all bytes and return the result only at the end.

MACs use shared secret keys: both parties can create and verify. Digital signatures use asymmetric keys: only the signer can create, anyone with the public key can verify. MACs provide authentication but not non-repudiation (either party could have created the MAC).

In many protocols, some data must be authenticated but not encrypted (headers, metadata). AEAD modes (GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) authenticate both encrypted data and plaintext associated data in a single operation, preventing header manipulation attacks.

learning_objectives: Derive RSA key generation from Euler's theorem and the Chinese Remainder Theorem
Perform RSA encryption and decryption on small numbers by hand
Understand RSA-OAEP padding and why textbook RSA is insecure
Analyze RSA key size recommendations relative to computational advances
readings: Boneh, Twenty Years of Attacks on the RSA Cryptosystem (survey)
NIST SP 800-56B: Recommendations for Pair-Wise Key Establishment Using Integer Factorization Cryptography

lecture_notes.introduction: RSA, published in 1977 by Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman, was the first practical public-key cryptosystem and remains the most widely deployed. Its security rests on the difficulty of factoring the product of two large prime numbers. Key generation selects two large primes p and q, computes n = p*q and phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1), chooses a public exponent e (commonly 65537), and computes the private exponent d = e^(-1) mod phi(n) using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm. Encryption is C = M^e mod n; decryption is M = C^d mod n. The mathematical beauty is that computing d from (n, e) requires factoring n into p and q, which is believed to be computationally hard for sufficiently large n. However, textbook RSA (direct modular exponentiation) is deterministic and malleable, making it insecure for real use. OAEP (Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding) adds randomness before encryption, making RSA-OAEP provably secure under the RSA assumption. RSA is too slow for bulk encryption (1000x slower than AES), so in practice it encrypts only symmetric keys (hybrid encryption) or creates digital signatures by signing hash digests.
key_concepts: RSA key generation: select primes p, q; compute n = p*q; compute phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1); choose e coprime to phi(n); compute d = e^(-1) mod phi(n)
RSA encryption: C = M^e mod n (fast with small e=65537, only 17 multiplications using square-and-multiply)
RSA decryption: M = C^d mod n (slower because d is large, accelerated with CRT: compute mod p and mod q separately, combine)
Textbook RSA weaknesses: deterministic (same plaintext always produces same ciphertext), malleable (E(m1) * E(m2) = E(m1*m2))
RSA-OAEP: pad plaintext with random value and hash before encryption, makes RSA semantically secure
Key size recommendations: RSA-2048 minimum (112-bit security), RSA-3072 for 128-bit security, RSA-4096 for long-term
Factoring records: RSA-829 (250 digits) factored in 2020 using 2700 CPU-years, RSA-2048 estimated to require 10^20 operations
Chinese Remainder Theorem optimization: private key operations mod p and mod q are 4x faster than mod n
RSA signature: Sign(m) = H(m)^d mod n, Verify(m, s) = check s^e mod n == H(m)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Perform RSA key generation by hand with small primes (p=61, q=53): compute n=3233, phi=3120, e=17, d=2753. Encrypt M=65 to get C=65^17 mod 3233 = 2790. Decrypt C=2790^2753 mod 3233 = 65. Then demonstrate textbook RSA malleability: show that multiplying two ciphertexts gives the ciphertext of the product. Finally, use OpenSSL to generate a real RSA-2048 key pair and examine its structure.
setup_requirements: Python for modular arithmetic computations
OpenSSL for RSA key generation and inspection
Calculator or Python for hand-computation verification
RSA-OAEP implementation example

review.value: RSA security relies on the difficulty of factoring n = p * q when p and q are each ~1024 bits. The best classical algorithm (General Number Field Sieve) has sub-exponential complexity L_n[1/3, 1.923]. Doubling the key size more than squares the factoring effort.

The public exponent e = 65537 = 2^16 + 1 is standard because: (1) it has only two 1-bits, making modular exponentiation fast (17 squarings, 1 multiplication), (2) it is large enough to prevent small-exponent attacks, (3) it is prime, simplifying the coprimality requirement.

Textbook RSA is a trap that appears in many insecure implementations. Without OAEP padding, RSA is deterministic (reveals repeated messages), malleable (attackers can manipulate ciphertext meaningfully), and vulnerable to chosen-ciphertext attacks.

RSA encrypts at most (key_size - padding_overhead) bytes. For RSA-2048 with OAEP, maximum plaintext is 190 bytes. To encrypt larger data, generate a random AES key, encrypt data with AES-GCM, encrypt the AES key with RSA-OAEP. This combines RSA's key distribution advantage with AES's speed.

class_schedule.topic: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange and Elliptic Curves
learning_objectives: Derive the Diffie-Hellman key exchange protocol from the discrete logarithm problem
Explain the man-in-the-middle attack on unauthenticated DH and how to prevent it
Understand elliptic curve mathematics and why ECC provides equivalent security with smaller keys
Compare ECDH with classical DH for modern key agreement
readings: Understanding Cryptography, Ch. 8 (Diffie-Hellman and Discrete Logarithm)
RFC 7748: Elliptic Curves for Security (X25519, X448)

lecture_notes.introduction: The Diffie-Hellman key exchange, published in 1976, was the breakthrough that launched public-key cryptography. It allows two parties who have never communicated before to establish a shared secret over a public channel. Alice and Bob agree on a large prime p and a generator g. Alice picks a random secret a and sends g^a mod p to Bob. Bob picks a random secret b and sends g^b mod p to Alice. Both compute the shared secret s = g^(ab) mod p: Alice computes (g^b)^a and Bob computes (g^a)^b. An eavesdropper who sees g^a and g^b cannot compute g^(ab) without solving the discrete logarithm problem, which is believed to be computationally hard. However, DH is vulnerable to a man-in-the-middle attack: an attacker can perform separate key exchanges with Alice and Bob, relaying modified messages. Authentication (digital signatures, certificates) is required to prevent this. Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH) replaces the multiplicative group mod p with the group of points on an elliptic curve, achieving equivalent security with much smaller keys. X25519, a Diffie-Hellman function on Curve25519, is the standard for modern key agreement in TLS 1.3, Signal, and WireGuard.
key_concepts: Discrete logarithm problem: given g, p, and g^a mod p, finding a is computationally hard
DH protocol: Alice sends A = g^a mod p, Bob sends B = g^b mod p, shared secret K = A^b = B^a = g^(ab) mod p
Man-in-the-middle attack: attacker performs separate DH exchanges with each party, must be prevented with authentication
Forward secrecy: using ephemeral DH keys ensures that compromise of long-term keys does not reveal past session keys
Elliptic curve group: points on y^2 = x^3 + ax + b over a finite field, with point addition as the group operation
ECDH: replace g^a with scalar multiplication aG (a times the generator point G), shared secret is abG
Key size comparison: 256-bit ECC provides security equivalent to 3072-bit RSA or 3072-bit DH
Curve25519/X25519: Montgomery curve designed for secure, constant-time implementation, standard in TLS 1.3
Static vs ephemeral DH: static DH reuses keys and provides no forward secrecy; ephemeral DH (DHE/ECDHE) generates new keys per session
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a simplified Diffie-Hellman key exchange in Python: Alice and Bob agree on p and g, generate random private keys, exchange public values, and compute the shared secret. Then demonstrate the man-in-the-middle attack by inserting Mallory between them. Finally, compare the speed and key sizes of DH-3072 vs ECDH-X25519 using OpenSSL benchmarks.
setup_requirements: Python with the cryptography library for ECDH
OpenSSL for DH and ECDH key generation and benchmarking
Network simulation for MitM demonstration
Wireshark for observing ECDHE in a TLS handshake

review.value: If a server's RSA private key is compromised, all past TLS sessions using RSA key exchange can be decrypted from recorded traffic. With ephemeral DH (DHE/ECDHE), each session uses a new DH key pair that is discarded after the session, so past sessions cannot be decrypted even if the long-term key is compromised.

A 256-bit ECC key provides equivalent security to a 3072-bit RSA key. This means faster key generation (milliseconds vs seconds), faster key exchange, smaller certificates, and lower bandwidth. ECC dominates modern cryptographic deployments.

Designed by Daniel Bernstein for security and implementation simplicity. Montgomery ladder ensures constant-time scalar multiplication (no timing side channels). Cofactor 8 prevents small-subgroup attacks. Widely adopted: TLS 1.3, Signal, WireGuard, SSH.

Raw DH provides no authentication. In TLS 1.3, the server signs its ephemeral DH public key with its RSA or ECDSA private key (from the certificate). The client verifies the signature, preventing MitM. This combination provides both authentication and forward secrecy.

class_schedule.topic: Digital Signatures: DSA, ECDSA, and EdDSA
learning_objectives: Explain the security model and applications of digital signatures
Compare DSA, ECDSA, and EdDSA in terms of security and performance
Understand the critical importance of randomness in DSA/ECDSA signing
Implement signature generation and verification using OpenSSL and Python

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital signatures provide authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation for digital messages and documents. Unlike MACs which use shared keys, digital signatures use asymmetric cryptography: the signer uses their private key to create the signature, and anyone with the corresponding public key can verify it. Non-repudiation means the signer cannot deny having signed the message, since only they possess the private key. DSA (Digital Signature Algorithm), published by NIST in 1991, uses the discrete logarithm problem in a subgroup of Z_p. ECDSA applies the same algorithm over elliptic curve groups for smaller keys and faster operations. Both DSA and ECDSA have a critical vulnerability: they require a unique random nonce k for each signature. If k is ever reused, the private key can be recovered from two signatures. This is not a theoretical concern -- Sony's PlayStation 3 code signing was broken because they used a constant k. EdDSA (Edwards-curve Digital Signature Algorithm), standardized as Ed25519, eliminates this vulnerability by deriving the nonce deterministically from the private key and message, making it impossible to reuse a nonce by accident.
key_concepts: Digital signature properties: authentication (verifier knows who signed), integrity (message not modified), non-repudiation (signer cannot deny)
Sign-then-verify: Signer computes sigma = Sign(sk, H(m)), Verifier checks Verify(pk, m, sigma) = true/false
DSA: based on discrete logarithm in subgroup of Z_p, requires random nonce k per signature
ECDSA: DSA over elliptic curves, 256-bit key provides 128-bit security, used in Bitcoin and TLS
Nonce reuse catastrophe: if k is reused in two ECDSA signatures (s1, s2 with same r), the private key d = (s1*H(m2) - s2*H(m1)) / (s1 - s2) mod n
PlayStation 3 hack: Sony used the same k for all signatures, allowing recovery of their private signing key from any two signatures
EdDSA (Ed25519): deterministic nonce k = H(secret_key_half || message), 128-bit security, constant-time, batch-verifiable
EdDSA advantages: deterministic (no random nonce needed), fast (single scalar multiplication for signing), resistant to side-channel attacks
Signature applications: code signing, certificate signing, document signing, blockchain transactions, software updates
lecture_notes.demonstration: Generate ECDSA and Ed25519 key pairs using OpenSSL and Python. Sign a message with both and verify. Then demonstrate the ECDSA nonce reuse attack: sign two different messages with the same k value and recover the private key from the two signatures. Finally, implement an Ed25519 signature and show that its deterministic nonce makes this attack impossible.
setup_requirements: Python with cryptography library for EdDSA and ECDSA
OpenSSL for key generation and signing operations
ECDSA nonce reuse exploit script (provided)
Wireshark for observing signature verification in TLS

review.value: If two signatures (r, s1) and (r, s2) share the same r (which happens when k is the same), then s1 - s2 = k^(-1)(H(m1) - H(m2)), so k = (H(m1) - H(m2)) / (s1 - s2). Then d = (s1*k - H(m1)) / r. The private key is completely exposed.

Ed25519 can perform ~71,000 signature verifications per second on a modern CPU. Signing is even faster. Batch verification of n signatures is faster than n individual verifications. This makes Ed25519 ideal for high-throughput systems.

Operating systems trust code signed by a known developer certificate chain. The developer signs the binary hash with their private key. The OS verifies the signature using the public key in the developer's certificate, which is itself signed by a trusted CA.

Shor's algorithm breaks DSA, ECDSA, and EdDSA. NIST has standardized ML-DSA (CRYSTALS-Dilithium) as the post-quantum replacement. Unlike Shor-vulnerable schemes, ML-DSA is based on lattice problems believed to be hard for quantum computers.

learning_objectives: Describe the PKI hierarchy: root CAs, intermediate CAs, and end-entity certificates
Inspect and validate X.509 certificate chains using OpenSSL
Identify certificate misuse: expired certificates, domain mismatches, revoked certificates
Understand Certificate Transparency and its role in detecting misissued certificates
readings: RFC 5280: Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile

lecture_notes.introduction: Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) solves the key distribution problem: how do you know that a public key actually belongs to the entity it claims to represent? Without PKI, an attacker could substitute their own public key for the legitimate server's, enabling man-in-the-middle attacks against every cryptographic protocol. PKI uses Certificate Authorities (CAs) that are trusted third parties. A CA verifies the identity of a certificate applicant and issues a digitally signed X.509 certificate binding their identity to their public key. Your browser trusts ~150 root CAs whose certificates are pre-installed in the operating system or browser. When a server presents its certificate, the browser validates the signature chain back to a trusted root. This system has significant weaknesses: any trusted CA can issue a certificate for any domain, a compromised CA can enable mass interception, and revocation checking is unreliable. Certificate Transparency (CT) addresses some of these issues by requiring CAs to publicly log all issued certificates, enabling domain owners to detect unauthorized certificate issuance. Understanding PKI is essential because it is the trust foundation for all secure internet communication.
key_concepts: X.509 certificate fields: version, serial number, issuer, validity period, subject, public key, extensions, signature
Subject Alternative Name (SAN): the extension that specifies which domain names the certificate is valid for
Certificate chain validation: end-entity cert signed by intermediate CA, intermediate signed by root CA, root is self-signed and in the trust store
Root CA trust store: ~150 pre-installed root certificates in OS/browser, any root can issue a cert for any domain
Certificate revocation: CRL (Certificate Revocation List) and OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol), OCSP stapling for performance
Certificate Transparency (CT): public append-only logs of all issued certificates, SCTs (Signed Certificate Timestamps) prove logging
Certificate pinning: application remembers the expected certificate or CA, rejects unexpected changes (deprecated in browsers but used in mobile apps)
Let's Encrypt: free automated CA using ACME protocol, issues DV (Domain Validation) certificates, responsible for >50% of HTTPS certificates
CA compromise impact: DigiNotar hack (2011) resulted in fake Google certificates used for MitM in Iran, entire CA was revoked and went bankrupt
lecture_notes.demonstration: Use OpenSSL to inspect a real website's certificate chain: examine the end-entity certificate's SAN, validity period, and signature algorithm. Verify the chain manually by checking each certificate's signature with its issuer's public key. Then use Certificate Transparency logs (crt.sh) to search for all certificates issued for a domain, and demonstrate how a domain owner can detect unauthorized certificates.
setup_requirements: OpenSSL for certificate inspection and chain verification
Browser developer tools for examining HTTPS certificate details
crt.sh access for Certificate Transparency log searches
Lab CA setup for issuing test certificates

review.value: 1) Verify end-entity cert signature with intermediate CA public key. 2) Verify intermediate cert signature with root CA public key. 3) Root CA is in trust store. 4) Check validity period. 5) Check SAN matches requested domain. 6) Check revocation via OCSP stapling.

CAs must log every issued certificate to multiple independent CT logs. Browsers require SCTs (proof of logging) in the certificate or TLS handshake. Domain owners monitor CT logs for unexpected certificates. This caught Symantec issuing unauthorized certificates, leading to Symantec's removal as a trusted CA.

Before Let's Encrypt (2016), HTTPS certificates cost $50-300/year and required manual installation. Let's Encrypt automated the process with free 90-day certificates, driving HTTPS adoption from ~40% to >90% of web traffic. The ACME protocol it created is now an IETF standard.

Any of ~150 trusted root CAs can issue a certificate for any domain. Compromising one CA (DigiNotar, Comodo) enables MitM attacks against the entire internet. Certificate Transparency, DANE/TLSA, and CAA DNS records mitigate but do not eliminate this risk.

learning_objectives: Analyze the TLS 1.3 handshake at the protocol level with all cryptographic operations
Compare TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3 in terms of latency, security, and cipher suites
Identify common TLS misconfigurations and historical attacks
Configure a secure TLS deployment using best practices
readings: RFC 8446: The Transport Layer Security (TLS) Protocol Version 1.3

lecture_notes.introduction: TLS (Transport Layer Security) is the protocol that encrypts virtually all internet traffic. Every HTTPS connection, every secure email, every VPN tunnel, and most API calls use TLS. Understanding TLS at the protocol level is essential for anyone working in security or networked systems. TLS 1.3, published in 2018, was a major redesign that removed insecure algorithms, reduced handshake latency from 2-RTT to 1-RTT (and 0-RTT for resumption), and made forward secrecy mandatory. The TLS 1.3 handshake begins with the client sending supported cipher suites and key shares (ECDH public values) in the ClientHello. The server responds with its chosen cipher suite, key share, and certificate in the ServerHello and subsequent messages, all encrypted after the key share exchange. The entire handshake completes in one round trip, compared to two for TLS 1.2. Historical TLS attacks (BEAST, POODLE, Heartbleed, DROWN, ROBOT) exploited weaknesses in older protocol versions and cipher suites that TLS 1.3 eliminated entirely.
key_concepts: TLS 1.3 handshake: ClientHello (supported groups, key shares, cipher suites) -> ServerHello (selected group, key share) -> encrypted extensions, certificate, CertificateVerify, Finished
1-RTT handshake: client sends key share in ClientHello, server responds with key share and encrypted data in ServerHello, application data flows after one round trip
0-RTT resumption: client sends encrypted application data with the ClientHello using a pre-shared key from a previous session (replay attack risk)
TLS 1.3 cipher suites: only 5 allowed (TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384, TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256, TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256, etc.)
Removed in TLS 1.3: RSA key exchange (no forward secrecy), CBC cipher suites (padding oracle), RC4, DES/3DES, MD5, SHA-1, static DH
Forward secrecy: mandatory in TLS 1.3 via ephemeral ECDHE, session keys cannot be recovered from long-term key compromise
Historical attacks: BEAST (CBC IV prediction), POODLE (CBC padding in SSLv3), Heartbleed (OpenSSL buffer over-read), DROWN (SSLv2 cross-protocol), ROBOT (RSA padding oracle)
Certificate verification in TLS: server proves identity by signing a transcript hash with its private key (CertificateVerify message)

Server Name Indication (SNI): client sends the target hostname in plaintext in ClientHello, enabling virtual hosting but leaking destination (Encrypted Client Hello in TLS 1.3 extension addresses this)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Capture a TLS 1.3 handshake with Wireshark using SSLKEYLOGFILE to decrypt the traffic. Examine each message in the handshake: ClientHello key shares, ServerHello response, encrypted extensions, certificate chain, and CertificateVerify signature. Then test a server's TLS configuration with testssl.sh, identifying weak cipher suites, protocol versions, and certificate issues. Finally, configure nginx with the Mozilla SSL Configuration Generator for a modern, secure deployment.
setup_requirements: Wireshark with TLS decryption (SSLKEYLOGFILE set in browser)
testssl.sh or sslyze for TLS server scanning
nginx with configurable TLS for secure deployment exercise
Mozilla SSL Configuration Generator access

review.value: TLS 1.3 eliminates entire classes of attacks by removing insecure options: no RSA key exchange (prevents ROBOT), no CBC modes (prevents POODLE/BEAST), no static DH (prevents LogJam), no MD5/SHA-1 (prevents transcript collision). Security by removing footguns.

TLS 1.3 0-RTT data can be replayed by an attacker: if the client sends a payment request in 0-RTT, an attacker can replay it. Servers must implement replay protection (single-use tickets, idempotent handling) or reject 0-RTT for state-changing requests.

CVE-2014-0160: a missing bounds check in OpenSSL's heartbeat extension allowed remote attackers to read 64KB of server memory per request, potentially exposing private keys, session keys, and user data. Affected ~17% of all HTTPS servers. Demonstrated the danger of memory-unsafe languages in security-critical code.

SNI in TLS reveals the destination hostname in plaintext, enabling censorship and surveillance. Encrypted Client Hello (ECH) encrypts the ClientHello using a public key published in DNS (via HTTPS record). When widely deployed, it will prevent SNI-based traffic filtering.

learning_objectives: Implement full-disk encryption with LUKS on Linux and understand BitLocker on Windows
Compare encryption granularity: full-disk, partition, file-level, and container
Analyze key management challenges: key derivation, secure storage, recovery
Evaluate the security of encrypted-at-rest data against various attack scenarios
readings: NIST SP 800-111: Guide to Storage Encryption Technologies

lecture_notes.introduction: Data-at-rest encryption protects stored data from unauthorized access when physical security is bypassed -- a stolen laptop, a decommissioned hard drive, or unauthorized physical access to a server. Full-disk encryption (FDE) is the most comprehensive approach: every sector on the disk is encrypted, including the operating system, swap space, and temporary files. LUKS (Linux Unified Key Setup) is the standard for Linux, using dm-crypt in the kernel. BitLocker is the Windows equivalent, using AES-XTS and integrating with TPM (Trusted Platform Module) for pre-boot authentication. Key management is the central challenge: the master encryption key must be derived from a user passphrase (using PBKDF2 or Argon2), protected by hardware (TPM), and recoverable in case of forgotten passwords. The choice of encryption mode matters: XTS mode is designed specifically for disk encryption because it handles the unique requirement of encrypting fixed-size sectors independently while preventing certain manipulation attacks. Understanding disk encryption is essential for compliance (HIPAA, GDPR, PCI-DSS all require encryption of sensitive data at rest) and for protecting against the most common data breach vector: lost or stolen devices.
key_concepts: Full-disk encryption (FDE): every sector encrypted, transparent to applications, protects against physical theft and forensic recovery
LUKS architecture: LUKS header stores key slots (up to 8), each slot encrypts the master key with a different passphrase via PBKDF2/Argon2
dm-crypt: Linux kernel subsystem that provides transparent encryption below the filesystem layer
BitLocker: Windows FDE using AES-128-XTS or AES-256-XTS, TPM integration for seamless boot, recovery key for emergencies
XTS mode: tweakable encryption mode for disk sectors, each sector encrypted with a tweak derived from sector number, prevents ECB-like pattern leakage
Key derivation: PBKDF2 (older, LUKS1) or Argon2id (recommended, LUKS2) converts passphrase to encryption key with tunable time/memory cost
TPM (Trusted Platform Module): hardware security module that seals the disk encryption key to the boot configuration, prevents offline attacks
Cold boot attack: DRAM retains data for seconds after power off; encryption keys can be recovered from memory. Mitigated by memory encryption (AMD SME/SEV)
File-level encryption: eCryptfs, fscrypt (ext4), EFS (Windows); encrypts individual files, finer granularity but metadata (filenames, sizes, directory structure) may leak
lecture_notes.demonstration: Set up LUKS2 encryption on a USB drive: create the LUKS container with cryptsetup luksFormat using Argon2id key derivation, add a second key slot with a recovery key, create a filesystem inside the encrypted container, mount and unmount it. Then demonstrate the cold boot attack concept by examining memory contents of a running LUKS system. Compare LUKS2 header structure with a BitLocker-encrypted drive image.
setup_requirements: Linux VM with cryptsetup 2.x installed
Spare USB drive or disk image file for LUKS setup
BitLocker encrypted drive image for analysis
TPM simulator or documentation for TPM integration discussion

review.value: LUKS stores the master key encrypted separately under each key slot's passphrase. Changing a passphrase only re-encrypts the master key under the new passphrase -- it does not re-encrypt the entire disk. This is why LUKS supports multiple passphrases and fast passphrase changes.

XTS encrypts each 16-byte block with a tweak derived from the sector number and block position. This means identical plaintext at different disk locations produces different ciphertext. It prevents an attacker from detecting moved or copied disk sectors.

An attacker with physical access modifies the unencrypted bootloader to capture the passphrase on next boot. Defense: Secure Boot verifies bootloader integrity via UEFI signature chain. TPM measured boot detects bootloader tampering and refuses to unseal the encryption key.

HIPAA: encrypted PHI is exempt from breach notification (safe harbor). GDPR: encryption is a recommended technical measure under Article 32. PCI-DSS: requires encryption of stored cardholder data. These regulations drive universal adoption of disk encryption.

learning_objectives: Compare database encryption strategies: TDE, column-level, and application-level encryption
Understand the performance and query implications of each encryption approach
Design a key management architecture for encrypted databases
Evaluate database encryption against specific threat models
readings: NIST SP 800-111: Guide to Storage Encryption Technologies (database section)
Microsoft SQL Server Always Encrypted architecture

lecture_notes.introduction: Databases contain an organization's most sensitive data: customer records, financial transactions, health information, and authentication credentials. Database encryption adds a layer of protection beyond access control, ensuring that data remains confidential even if the database server is compromised, backups are stolen, or an administrator goes rogue. Three strategies exist at different layers. Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) encrypts the database files at the storage layer, transparent to applications and queries, protecting against physical theft of database files or backups. Column-level encryption encrypts specific sensitive columns (SSN, credit card numbers) within the database, protecting against database administrator access and SQL injection that reads data. Application-level encryption encrypts data before it reaches the database, providing the strongest protection because even the database server never sees plaintext, but sacrificing the ability to query encrypted data. Each approach has different threat models, performance characteristics, and query limitations. The key management architecture -- where keys are stored, how they are rotated, and who has access -- is often more important than the encryption algorithm itself.
key_concepts: Transparent Data Encryption (TDE): encrypts database files at rest, transparent to queries and applications, protects against physical media theft
TDE key hierarchy: master key (in HSM or key vault) encrypts database encryption key (DEK), DEK encrypts data files and backups
Column-level encryption: pgcrypto (PostgreSQL), Always Encrypted (SQL Server), encrypt specific columns, queryable only with key
Application-level encryption: application encrypts before INSERT, decrypts after SELECT, database never sees plaintext
Searchable encryption: homomorphic encryption, order-preserving encryption, and deterministic encryption allow limited queries on encrypted data (with trade-offs)
Key management: Hardware Security Modules (HSMs), AWS KMS / Azure Key Vault / GCP Cloud KMS for key storage and rotation
Key rotation: TDE supports re-encryption with new key, column-level requires UPDATE of all encrypted values, application-level handles rotation in application code
Performance impact: TDE ~2-5% overhead (AES-NI acceleration), column-level 5-20% per encrypted column, application-level varies by implementation
Threat model comparison: TDE protects against disk theft, column-level protects against DBA access, application-level protects against database compromise
lecture_notes.demonstration: Set up column-level encryption in PostgreSQL using pgcrypto: create a table with an encrypted SSN column, insert data with pgp_sym_encrypt(), query with pgp_sym_decrypt(), and show that a direct SELECT without the key returns ciphertext. Then compare query performance on encrypted vs unencrypted columns and discuss the inability to index or search encrypted columns efficiently.
setup_requirements: PostgreSQL with pgcrypto extension enabled
Sample database with sensitive columns to encrypt
Performance benchmarking queries
Key management diagram template

review.value: TDE protects only against physical theft of database files and backups. It does NOT protect against: SQL injection (data is decrypted for queries), compromised DBA accounts (TDE is transparent to authorized connections), or memory dumps (data is plaintext in memory during query execution).

SQL Server Always Encrypted keeps the encryption key on the client. The database server processes queries on encrypted data without decrypting. Supports deterministic encryption (allows equality comparisons) and randomized encryption (no query support but stronger security).

review.key: Key Management is the Hard Part
review.value: The encryption algorithm is the easy part. Key management -- secure generation, storage, distribution, rotation, revocation, and destruction -- is where most deployments fail. Hardcoded keys, keys in config files, and keys accessible to too many people are the most common vulnerabilities.

Encrypt data with a data encryption key (DEK). Encrypt the DEK with a key encryption key (KEK) stored in an HSM/KMS. Store the encrypted DEK alongside the data. To rotate the KEK, re-encrypt only the DEK (seconds), not all the data (hours/days). AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, and GCP Cloud KMS all use this pattern.

learning_objectives: Explain Shor's algorithm and its threat to RSA, DH, and ECC
Evaluate NIST post-quantum standards: ML-KEM (CRYSTALS-Kyber) and ML-DSA (CRYSTALS-Dilithium)
Understand lattice-based cryptography and the Learning With Errors problem
Plan a post-quantum migration strategy for existing systems
readings: NIST Post-Quantum Cryptography Standardization: Final Standards announcement
NIST FIPS 204: ML-DSA (Module-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Algorithm)

lecture_notes.introduction: Quantum computers exploit quantum mechanical phenomena (superposition and entanglement) to solve certain problems exponentially faster than classical computers. Shor's algorithm, published in 1994, factors large integers and computes discrete logarithms in polynomial time, breaking RSA, Diffie-Hellman, and all elliptic curve cryptography. While no quantum computer yet has enough qubits to threaten current key sizes (breaking RSA-2048 requires approximately 4,000 logical qubits or millions of physical qubits), the threat is real for two reasons: cryptographic systems must protect data for decades, and harvest-now-decrypt-later attacks mean adversaries recording encrypted traffic today could decrypt it once quantum computers become available. NIST completed its Post-Quantum Cryptography standardization in 2024, selecting three algorithms: ML-KEM (CRYSTALS-Kyber) for key encapsulation, ML-DSA (CRYSTALS-Dilithium) for digital signatures, and SLH-DSA (SPHINCS+) as a hash-based signature backup. All three primary schemes are based on lattice problems, specifically the Module Learning With Errors (MLWE) problem, which is believed to be hard for both classical and quantum computers. The transition to post-quantum cryptography is the largest cryptographic migration in history and will take a decade or more.
key_concepts: Shor's algorithm: factors n in O((log n)^3) using quantum Fourier transform, breaks RSA and DH in polynomial time
Grover's algorithm: searches unsorted database in O(sqrt(N)), halves the effective security of symmetric ciphers (AES-128 -> 64-bit security, AES-256 -> 128-bit security)
Harvest-now-decrypt-later: adversaries record encrypted traffic now to decrypt when quantum computers become available, making migration urgent for sensitive data
Lattice-based cryptography: security based on the hardness of finding short vectors in high-dimensional lattices (Shortest Vector Problem, Learning With Errors)
ML-KEM (CRYSTALS-Kyber): lattice-based key encapsulation, three security levels (ML-KEM-512, 768, 1024), public key ~800-1568 bytes, ciphertext ~768-1568 bytes
ML-DSA (CRYSTALS-Dilithium): lattice-based digital signatures, three levels, signature ~2420-4627 bytes (larger than ECDSA's ~64 bytes)
SLH-DSA (SPHINCS+): hash-based signature scheme, no lattice assumptions, slower and larger but based only on hash function security
Hybrid key exchange: combine classical (ECDH) with post-quantum (ML-KEM) so the connection is secure if either algorithm holds, used in Chrome and Cloudflare's TLS
Migration challenges: larger key and signature sizes, performance differences, algorithm agility in protocols, updating embedded devices
lecture_notes.demonstration: Use the liboqs (Open Quantum Safe) library to generate ML-KEM-768 key pairs, encapsulate and decapsulate a shared secret, and benchmark against X25519 ECDH. Then generate ML-DSA-65 signatures and compare size and speed with Ed25519. Finally, establish a hybrid TLS connection using OpenSSL with OQS provider, combining X25519 with ML-KEM-768 for key exchange.
setup_requirements: liboqs library installed (Open Quantum Safe)
Python with oqs-python bindings for PQC algorithm access
OpenSSL with OQS provider for hybrid TLS testing
Benchmark comparison scripts for classical vs PQC algorithms

review.value: Current quantum computers have ~1,000 physical qubits. Breaking RSA-2048 requires ~4,000 logical qubits, each requiring ~1,000 physical qubits for error correction = ~4 million physical qubits. Most estimates place this at 15-30 years, but harvest-now-decrypt-later means migration should start now.

ML-KEM-768 public key: 1,184 bytes (vs X25519: 32 bytes). ML-DSA-65 signature: 3,309 bytes (vs Ed25519: 64 bytes). This size increase impacts TLS handshake size, certificate chains, and bandwidth-constrained environments.

Post-quantum algorithms are newer and less battle-tested than classical algorithms. Hybrid mode combines both: the shared secret is derived from both X25519 and ML-KEM. If ML-KEM is broken by classical cryptanalysis, X25519 still protects. If quantum computers break X25519, ML-KEM still protects.

Grover's algorithm only halves the effective security of symmetric ciphers. AES-256 retains 128-bit security against quantum attacks, which is sufficient. No changes needed for AES-256 or SHA-256 (output > 256 bits). The migration is entirely about replacing asymmetric cryptography.

class_schedule.topic: Cryptographic Implementation Lab and Course Integration
learning_objectives: Identify common cryptographic implementation mistakes and their consequences
Apply defense-in-depth principles to cryptographic system design
Evaluate real-world cryptographic architectures for correctness and security
Synthesize all course topics into a comprehensive cryptographic security assessment
readings: Review all assigned textbook chapters and NIST publications
Class notes and assignments from weeks 1-14

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session brings together every cryptographic concept we have studied by examining how they combine in real-world systems and where implementations commonly go wrong. The most dangerous cryptographic vulnerabilities are not broken algorithms but broken implementations: using ECB mode when CBC or GCM was needed, reusing nonces in GCM or ECDSA, failing to validate certificate chains, using weak key derivation for passwords, or hardcoding encryption keys in source code. We will walk through a comprehensive security audit of a hypothetical secure messaging application, examining each cryptographic decision against the principles learned in this course. The application uses X25519 for key agreement, AES-256-GCM for message encryption, Ed25519 for message signing, Argon2id for password hashing, LUKS for local data encryption, and TLS 1.3 with certificate pinning for transport. For each component, we will evaluate whether the algorithm choice is correct, whether the implementation parameters are appropriate, and what could go wrong. This integration exercise prepares you for the real world where cryptographic decisions have direct security consequences.
key_concepts: Cryptographic right answers 2024: X25519 for key exchange, AES-256-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305 for encryption, Ed25519 for signatures, Argon2id for passwords, SHA-256 or BLAKE3 for hashing
Common implementation mistakes: nonce reuse (GCM/ECDSA), ECB mode, unauthenticated encryption, weak key derivation, timing side channels in comparison
Key management failures: hardcoded keys, keys in source control, keys accessible to too many services, no key rotation plan
Certificate validation failures: not checking hostname, not checking expiry, not checking revocation, trusting self-signed certificates in production
Cryptographic agility: designing systems so algorithms can be replaced without redesigning the entire system (important for PQC migration)
Defense in depth: TLS for transport + AES-GCM for message + LUKS for storage, so compromise of one layer does not expose all data
Secure random number generation: always use OS-provided CSPRNG (/dev/urandom, BCryptGenRandom), never use Math.random() or time-based seeds
Constant-time operations: MAC verification, password comparison, and key comparison must not branch on secret data to prevent timing attacks
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a live cryptographic audit of a provided web application's security architecture. Examine TLS configuration with testssl.sh, inspect password hashing implementation (Argon2id parameters), verify encryption mode usage (check for ECB, check nonce generation), review certificate validation code, and check for timing side channels in authentication. Score each component and produce a remediation report.
setup_requirements: Target web application for security audit (provided)
testssl.sh and sslyze for TLS assessment
Source code review tools
Review notes from all previous sessions

review.value: 1) Is encryption authenticated? (AEAD required) 2) Are nonces unique? (random or counter, never reused) 3) Is key derivation slow? (Argon2id for passwords) 4) Are keys properly stored? (HSM, KMS, or OS keychain) 5) Is TLS 1.3 with forward secrecy? 6) Are certificates validated correctly? 7) Is random generation from CSPRNG?

Application layer: Argon2id password hashing, AES-256-GCM message encryption. Transport layer: TLS 1.3 with ECDHE and certificate pinning. Storage layer: LUKS full-disk encryption with TPM. Each layer protects against different threats; together they provide defense in depth.

Begin collecting cryptographic inventory: identify all uses of RSA, DH, and ECC across the organization. Prioritize migration for long-lived secrets. Deploy hybrid key exchange in TLS. Plan certificate infrastructure upgrade to PQC signatures. This migration will take years.

Symmetric encryption (AES-GCM) for bulk data confidentiality, asymmetric encryption (RSA/ECDH) for key distribution, hash functions (SHA-256) for integrity, MACs (HMAC) for authentication, digital signatures (Ed25519) for non-repudiation, PKI (X.509) for identity, TLS for transport security, disk encryption for data at rest. Every topic in this course is a link in the chain of real-world security.

quizzes.topic: Symmetric Encryption and Block Cipher Modes

questions.question: A developer encrypts user data using AES-256-ECB and stores the ciphertext in a database. When two users submit the same Social Security Number, the ciphertext is identical. Explain why this is a vulnerability, what specific information it leaks, and which mode should be used instead with its implementation requirements.
questions.answer: ECB mode encrypts identical plaintext blocks to identical ciphertext blocks because it applies the block cipher independently with the same key. This leaks equality information: an attacker can determine when two records contain the same value without decryption. In a database context, this enables frequency analysis (finding the most common SSN by counting duplicate ciphertexts) and known-plaintext correlation attacks. Use AES-256-GCM instead, which requires a unique 96-bit nonce per encryption operation. GCM provides confidentiality (same plaintext produces different ciphertext each time due to the nonce) and authenticity (the authentication tag detects any tampering). Store the nonce alongside the ciphertext as the nonce is not secret, only unique.

rubric.key: Explains ECB deterministic encryption vulnerability
Identifies specific information leaked (equality, frequency analysis)
Recommends GCM with correct nonce requirements

questions.question: You discover that a file encryption application uses AES-256-GCM but generates nonces using a counter starting at 1, incremented for each file. The same key is used for all files encrypted by a user. A colleague argues this is fine because the nonces are unique. Is the colleague correct? What is the maximum number of files that can be safely encrypted this way, and what would you recommend instead?
questions.answer: The colleague is partially correct: counter-based nonces are valid for GCM as long as they never repeat with the same key. With a 96-bit nonce space, a counter allows 2^96 unique nonces, far more than any practical system needs. However, there are risks: if the counter state is lost (application crash, reinstallation) the counter may restart at 1, causing nonce reuse which is catastrophic for GCM (reveals auth key and allows plaintext recovery via XOR). If the application runs on multiple devices with the same key, counters may collide. A safer approach: use a 96-bit random nonce from a CSPRNG. The birthday bound for collision is at 2^48 (~281 trillion) encryptions, which is safe for most applications. For very high-volume systems, partition the nonce space: 32-bit device ID + 64-bit counter per device, ensuring no collisions across devices.

rubric.key: Correctly assesses counter nonces as valid but fragile
Identifies counter restart and multi-device collision risks
Recommends random nonce with birthday bound analysis or partitioned counter

questions.question: Explain the difference between a stream cipher and a block cipher in CTR mode. Are they functionally equivalent? What is one advantage of CTR mode over a dedicated stream cipher like ChaCha20?
questions.answer: Functionally, they are nearly equivalent: both generate a pseudorandom keystream and XOR it with plaintext. A stream cipher (ChaCha20) is purpose-built for stream generation. CTR mode converts a block cipher (AES) into a stream cipher by encrypting sequential counter values. The key difference is that CTR mode's security relies on the underlying block cipher (AES), which has decades of cryptanalysis. One advantage: CTR mode with AES benefits from AES-NI hardware acceleration available on virtually all modern x86 and ARM processors, achieving 5-10 GB/s throughput. ChaCha20 is faster in software without AES-NI (common on older mobile devices) but slower than hardware-accelerated AES. CTR mode also allows random access: to decrypt byte i, compute which counter block it falls in and decrypt just that block, without processing any preceding blocks.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies functional equivalence of keystream XOR approach
Explains the hardware acceleration advantage or random access advantage
Notes the security foundation difference

quizzes.topic: Hash Functions, MACs, and Key Exchange

questions.question: A password database was breached. Passwords were stored as unsalted SHA-256 hashes. Explain the specific attack that makes this dangerous, and describe how Argon2id with a 256MB memory cost, 3 iterations, and per-user 128-bit salt changes the attacker's calculus. Include specific throughput estimates.
questions.answer: Without salting, attackers use precomputed rainbow tables to reverse SHA-256 hashes instantly for common passwords. Identical passwords produce identical hashes, enabling batch attacks against all users simultaneously. SHA-256 is designed for speed: a modern GPU computes ~10 billion SHA-256 hashes per second. With Argon2id (256MB memory, 3 iterations, per-user salt): (1) Rainbow tables are useless because each user has a unique salt. (2) Each password must be attacked individually. (3) The 256MB memory requirement prevents GPU parallelism (a GPU with 8GB VRAM can run only ~32 Argon2id instances simultaneously vs millions of SHA-256). (4) Estimated throughput: ~4 Argon2id hashes/second per CPU core, ~100 per high-end GPU (vs 10 billion SHA-256). For a 10-character password with mixed case, digits, and symbols (~60^10 = 6 * 10^17 combinations), brute force would take ~190 million years per user at 100 hashes/second.

rubric.key: Identifies rainbow table and speed attacks on unsalted SHA-256
Explains per-user salt eliminating batch attacks
Quantifies the memory-hardness impact on GPU parallelism

questions.question: Explain why HMAC-SHA256(key, message) is secure but SHA256(key || message) is not. What specific attack does HMAC prevent, and how does its nested construction defeat the attack?
questions.answer: SHA-256 uses the Merkle-Damgard construction where the final hash state after processing message M is exposed as the hash output. An attacker who knows SHA256(key || message) and the length of (key || message) can compute SHA256(key || message || padding || extension) by continuing the hash computation from the known final state, without knowing the key. This length extension attack allows forging valid tags for extended messages. HMAC-SHA256 defeats this with its nested construction: HMAC(K,M) = SHA256((K XOR opad) || SHA256((K XOR ipad) || M)). The inner hash SHA256((K XOR ipad) || M) is exposed only to the outer hash, not to the attacker. The outer hash SHA256((K XOR opad) || inner_hash) takes a fixed-length input (512 bits), so there is nothing to extend. Even if an attacker could extend the inner hash, the outer hash would produce a completely different HMAC.

rubric.key: Correctly describes length extension attack mechanism
Explains how HMAC's nested construction prevents extension
Notes that outer hash takes fixed-length input

questions.question: During a Diffie-Hellman key exchange, Alice sends g^a mod p to Bob and Bob sends g^b mod p to Alice. Mallory intercepts both and performs separate exchanges. Write the exact values Mallory sends and receives, and explain why authentication with digital signatures prevents this attack.
questions.answer: Mallory intercepts A = g^a from Alice and B = g^b from Bob. Mallory generates her own secrets m1 and m2. She sends g^m1 to Bob (pretending to be Alice) and g^m2 to Alice (pretending to be Bob). Alice computes K_AM = (g^m2)^a = g^(a*m2). Mallory computes K_AM = (g^a)^m2 = g^(a*m2). Bob computes K_MB = (g^m1)^b = g^(b*m1). Mallory computes K_MB = (g^b)^m1 = g^(b*m1). Mallory now shares K_AM with Alice and K_MB with Bob. She decrypts Alice's messages with K_AM, reads them, re-encrypts with K_MB and sends to Bob (and vice versa). Authentication prevents this: if Alice signs her g^a with her private key, Bob can verify the signature with Alice's public key (from a certificate). Mallory cannot forge Alice's signature because she does not have Alice's private key. Bob will reject g^m1 because Mallory cannot sign it as Alice.

rubric.key: Correct MitM protocol with Mallory's values
Explains two separate shared secrets and relay capability
Explains how digital signatures on DH values prevent the attack
